chemistry.scope_summary: Primary example reaction used to demonstrate the laser CSTR platform for scaling photochemical reactions. Achieved 1.85 kg scale with 89% conversion and 1.2 kg/day throughput using a 100 mL reactor.

optimization_table.key_findings: Beer-Lambert law relationship between catalyst concentration and reaction rate; optimal power density identified for scale-up

figures.caption: Figure 1.  Comparison of CSTRs and PFRs.

figures.flow_path: Starting Materials flow into Laser Driven Flow Reactor to produce Products
figures.key_information: Faster Reactions, Less Catalyst, Flexible Platform, 1.8 kg Demonstration
figures.caption: Figure 1.  Comparison of CSTRs and PFRs.

figures.flow_path: reactants to products through either CSTR or PFR configurations
figures.key_information: Comparison diagram showing CSTR with uniform composition versus PFR with composition varying by position
figures.caption: Figure 1.  Comparison of CSTRs and PFRs.

Figure 2.  (A) C − N coupling reaction used with the optimized conditions. (B) Plot of the initial rates of reaction at several different catalyst concentrations with the proposed exponential relationship shown as a dotted line. (C) Time course plot of the C − N coupling reaction showing the conversion of aryl bromide in red and the solution darkening effect in blue.

Figure 3.  (A) Initial rates as a function of catalyst concentration demonstrating a Beer − Lambert law relationship in decarboxylative C − C bond formation. (B) Initial rates as a function of catalyst concentration as another demonstration of the Beer − Lambert law in the anti-Markovnikov addition of carboxylic acids to alkenes.

Figure 4.  (A) Correlation between initial rates in the C − N coupling and power density of the laser source where the standard reactor employed was 6.5 cm diameter (167 mL total volume), the large reactor was 8 cm (250 mL total volume), and the small reactor was 5 cm in diameter (100 mL). (B) Example reaction where the rate/power correlation breaks down providing an optimal power density for scal | Figure 5.  (A) Optimized reaction conditions determined for 1.85 kg flow reaction. (B) Kinetic time course for the reaction conditions shown in part A in a 100 mL vessel. (C) Levenspiel analysis of the reaction kinetics from part B. (D) Schematic of the CSTR used in the flow reaction.

figures.flow_path: Starting Material → Inlet Pump → reactor → Outlet Pump → Product
figures.key_information: Continuous flow photochemical reactor with fiber optic laser and beam expander for light distribution, yellow reaction mixture visible

translation_logic.batch_limitation: Lower throughput limited by reactor volume and batch processing requirements for photochemical reactions
translation_logic.flow_advantage: CSTR design enables kg-scale production with optimized light distribution and Beer-Lambert law relationship for catalyst efficiency
translation_logic.what_changed: Reactor type changed from batch to CSTR flow, temperature reduced from 80°C to 70°C, power increased from 25W to 26.1W with fiber optic laser
translation_logic.what_stayed_same: Concentration maintained at 0.8 M, same solvent (DMA), same wavelength (450 nm), same catalyst system
translation_logic.safety_improvement: Continuous flow processing reduces safety risks compared to large batch reactions

figure_descriptions: Reactor scheme showing CSTR configuration with fiber optic laser and beam expander
Optimization plots for catalyst concentration and power density relationships
Beer-Lambert law demonstrations for multiple reaction types

conflicts: Figure shows 1 inlet but pass3 indicates 2 inlets for pumps
Multiple reactor volumes mentioned (100 mL, 167 mL, 250 mL, 700 mL) - 100 mL selected as the optimized version
notes: Well-documented scale-up study with clear Beer-Lambert law optimization. Some inconsistencies in inlet numbers between figure and text descriptions. Throughput calculated from scale and residence time. Multiple reactor sizes discussed for optimization studies.
